coredata.dc:description: Metal oxides with peroxidase (POD)-like and halogen peroxidase (HPO)-like catalytic activities effectively eliminate bacteria by catalyzing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, including hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and hypobromous acid (HBrO)/hypochlorous acid (HClO). While metal phosphides generally exhibit higher catalytic activity than metal oxides, bimetallic compounds tend to show better catalytic activity than monometallic compounds. However, there is currently a lack of bimetallic phosphides nanozymes with enhanced POD- and HPO-like activities to selectively produce ·OH and HBrO/HClO for antibacterial applications. Here, FeCu–P/carbon dots (FeCu–P/CDs) nanozymes show multi-enzyme activity to catalyze ROS production for enhanced antibacterial activity. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrate POD-like, HPO-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities. POD-like activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with a specific activity of 37.9 U/mg is 8.2 times improved than Cu–P/CDs. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes show enhanced antibacterial activity by catalyzing the production of ·OH and HBrO/HClO through dual pathways, respectively. Based on the POD-like catalytic pathway, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes achieved antibacterial rates exceeding 99.9 % against E. coli and S. aureus at 25 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL, respectively, which are significantly higher than those of Cu–P/CDs and Fe–P/CDs. Based on the HPO-like catalytic pathway, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrated antibacterial effects against E. coli and S. aureus, achieving an antibacterial rate of 98.7 % against E. coli at 400 μg/mL. This work will inspire the exploration of a variety of bimetallic species-phosphate/carbon nanomaterials with high catalytic activity to enhance antibacterial properties via multi-enzyme activity pathway.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal oxides with peroxidase (POD)-like and halogen peroxidase (HPO)-like catalytic activities effectively eliminate bacteria by catalyzing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, including hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and hypobromous acid (HBrO)/hypochlorous acid (HClO). While metal phosphides generally exhibit higher catalytic activity than metal oxides, bimetallic compounds tend to show better catalytic activity than monometallic compounds. However, there is currently a lack of bimetallic phosphides nanozymes with enhanced POD- and HPO-like activities to selectively produce ·OH and HBrO/HClO for antibacterial applications. Here, FeCu–P/carbon dots (FeCu–P/CDs) nanozymes show multi-enzyme activity to catalyze ROS production for enhanced antibacterial activity. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrate POD-like, HPO-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities. POD-like activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with a specific activity of 37.9 U/mg is 8.2 times improved than Cu–P/CDs. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes show enhanced antibacterial activity by catalyzing the production of ·OH and HBrO/HClO through dual pathways, respectively. Based on the POD-like catalytic pathway, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes achieved antibacterial rates exceeding 99.9 % against E. coli and S. aureus at 25 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL, respectively, which are significantly higher than those of Cu–P/CDs and Fe–P/CDs. Based on the HPO-like catalytic pathway, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrated antibacterial effects against E. coli and S. aureus, achieving an antibacterial rate of 98.7 % against E. coli at 400 μg/mL. This work will inspire the exploration of a variety of bimetallic species-phosphate/carbon nanomaterials with high catalytic activity to enhance antibacterial properties via multi-enzyme activity pathway.

FeCu–P/carbon dots (FeCu–P/CDs) nanozymes possess multi-enzyme activity, such as POD-, HPO- and SOD-like activity. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes catalyze the production of ·OH and HBrO/HClO ROS through POD-like and HPO-like catalytic activity respectively, for the purpose of enhancing antibacterial activity. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes achieved an E. coli inhibition rate of over 99.9 % at 25 μg/mL via the POD-like catalytic pathway.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: To combat the public health threat posed by antibiotic-resistant pathogens, a great deal of effort has been put into exploring new nanomaterials with effective antibacterial properties to replace traditional antibiotics [1–3]. Among various nano-drugs, nanozymes have been widely used for elimination of bacteria and wound disinfection due to their simple preparation, tunable catalytic centers and cost-effectiveness compared to natural enzymes [4–7]. Typically, artificial antimicrobial agents based on nanozymes possess the ability to catalyze the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals (‧OH) and hypohalous acids, which can induce irreversible bacterial damage to achieve antibacterial properties [8–11]. Actually, ‧OH and hypobromic acid/hypochlorous acid (HBrO/HClO) are the most effective ROS against pathogens [12,13]. Therefore, the development of artificial nanozymes, which have the ability to selectively produce ‧OH and HBrO/HClO, may provide a promising direction for the development of novel biocatalysts to combat antibiotic-resistant pathogens.

Monometallic oxide nanomaterials exhibiting peroxidase (POD)-like or halogen peroxidase (HPO)-like catalytic activity possess diverse structures and adjustable catalytic centers, effectively catalyzing the generation of highly toxic ·OH and HBrO/HClO [14–16]. However, bimetallic nanozymes usually exhibit higher enzyme-like catalytic activity than monometallic nanozymes. This enhanced activity is attributed to the synergistic effect between the two metal elements and the fact that multiple metal ion valence states are more conducive to catalytic reactions at the active centers [17,18]. Based on this significantly enhanced catalytic activity, bimetallic nanozymes can generate ROS via enzyme-like catalysis, thereby achieving enhanced antibacterial activity. Importantly, ‧OH and HBrO/HClO are the most effective ROS to destroy pathogens, so it is of great scientific significance and application value to develop nanozymes with selective ·OH and HBrO/HClO production activities. V–Fe2O3 nanozymes with POD-like and HPO-like activities were constructed, which exhibit the ability to selectively generate ‧OH and HClO, and demonstrate excellent antibacterial properties [13]. Notably, metal phosphides have exhibited more excellent POD-like catalytic activity than oxides due to the special structure [19,20]. Further, as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) contain rich functional groups (-NH2, –COOH, –OH) on their surface, which can chelate with metal ions to form coordination bonds. The coordination bonds establish electronic interactions between the CDs substrate and the metal ions, thereby adjusting the electronic structure of the metal ions, optimizing the catalytic performance, and ultimately improving catalytic efficiency. However, there is currently a lack of bimetallic phosphides/CDs nanozymes with enhanced POD- and HPO-like activities to selectively produce ·OH and HBrO/HClO for antibacterial applications. According to our previous work, CDs-ferric ions (Fe3+) and CDs-copper ions (Cu2+) exhibited the dominant POD-like and HPO-like enzymes catalytic activities, respectively [21].

Herein, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with multi-enzyme activity are constructed using CDs-Fe3+ and CDs-Cu2+ as precursors, demonstrating POD-like, HPO-like and superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activities. Among them, SOD-like activity and POD-like activity constitute a cascade catalytic effect. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit POD-like activities with a specific activity of 37.9 U/mg, which is enhanced 0.9 and 8.2 times than that of Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs, respectively. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes can efficiently eliminate bacteria in both acidic and neutral environments by generating ·OH and HBrO/HClO through dual pathways, which mimic the activities of POD-like and HPO-like enzymes. Based on the POD-like catalytic pathway, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes achieve antibacterial rates exceeding 99.9 % against E. coli and S. aureus at 25 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL, respectively, in an acidic environment. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit enhanced antibacterial activity compared to Cu–P/CDs and Fe–P/CDs. In a neutral environment, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit antibacterial activity against both E. coli and S. aureus. In particular, the antibacterial rate of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes against E. coli is 98.7 % at 400 μg/mL via the HPO-like pathway, which is improved compared to Cu–P/CDs and FeCu–P. This work will inspire the exploration of a variety of bimetallic species-phosphate/carbon nanomaterials with high catalytic activity to enhance antibacterial properties via multi-enzyme activity pathway.

Chitosan (CS) and Acrylamide (AM) were purchased from Aladdin. EDTA was purchased from Tianda Chemical Reagent Factory. FeCl3 and CuCl2‧2H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ammonium persulfate (APS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Absolute ethanol was purchased from Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) was bought from Tianjin Beilian Chemicals Reagent Co. Xanthine oxidase, Xanthine and Dialysis bags (MWCO = 500 Da) were obtained from Solarbio (USA). 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Macklin. All solutions were prepared using deionized water from a Milli-Q water purification system with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-metals ions serve as precursors to synthesize FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes via phosphorization
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized according to the previous work, with the precursors being CS (0.4 g), AM (2.5 g), EDTA (1.4 g) and APS (0.01 g) [21]. Solution A: 21 mmol NaOH and 3 mmol Na2CO3 were dissolved in 40 mL water and then ultrasonicated for 5 min. Solution B: 1.5 mmol Cu2+ and Fe3+ were dissolved in CDs solution (3 mg/mL, 40 mL), respectively. Then CDs-Fe3+/CDs-Cu2+ solution was obtained by ultrasonic treatment of the two solution for 5 min. Subsequently, solution A was dropped into solution B until the pH was 10. Collected the precipitate and dried the product. Then, the bimetallic phosphide/CDs composites were synthesized by NaH2PO2 as phosphorus source in nitrogen atmosphere at 350 °C for 2 h. Finally, washed the product with deionized water and ethanol several times, then obtained FeCu–P/CDs. Similarly, FeCu–P nanozymes were synthesized by using deionized water instead of CDs solution. Fe–P/CDs nanozymes and Cu–P/CDs nanozymes were obtained using only ferric or copper ions as metal precursors, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase, morphology and surface element state of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) patterns were recorded to characterize the crystalline structures of FeCu–P/CDs, FeCu–P, Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs. The morphology of FeCu–P/CDs, FeCu–P and CDs was performed by Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The surface element state of FeCu–P/CDs, FeCu–P and CDs were confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, Al Kα). EPR spectroscopy was conducted on a Bruker spectrometer (A300).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-enzyme activity evaluation of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes

ce:section.ce:para: 150 μL TMB solution (20 mM), 120 μL H2O2 solution (50 mM) and 50 μL nanozymes solution (1 mg/mL) were added into a 3.0 mL tube containing 2680 μL of NaOAc/HOAc buffer (pH 3.5). The POD-like catalytic activity of nanozymes were evaluated by testing the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm.

Nanozymes, H2O2 and Br− were added to CB solution to initiate the reaction. A certain amount of each reactant was mixed in a phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) so that the final concentrations of CB, Br−, H2O2 and nanozymes were 50 μM, 0.1 mM, 1 mM and 16.7 μg/mL. The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of 400–800 nm were measured. The absorbance values at 520 nm and 640 nm were compared to evaluate the HPO-like relative catalytic activity of different nanozymes.

The Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum velocity (V m) were calculated based on the Michaelis-Menten saturation curve (1) [21,22]. Specific activity (SA) were calculated according to equation (2):
(1)

(2) SA = [V/(Ԑ × Ɩ) × (ΔA/Δt)]/m

V is the total volume of the reaction solution (μL); ε is the molar absorption coefficient of the colorimetric substrate (39000 M−1 cm−1 at 652 nm for TMB; 15200 M−1 cm−1 at 640 nm for CB); Ɩ is the path length of light traveling in the cuvette (cm); ΔA/Δt is the initial rate of change of absorbance at 652 nm or 640 nm; m is the nanozymes weight (mg) of each assay.

Xanthine was reacted with xanthine oxidase in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) to produce •O2 −, and then the nanozymes were added to the solution for incubation. Finally, INT was added to detect the content of •O2 −. The absorption peak at 505 nm was measured. The clearance rate of •O2 − is calculated by the following formula [23]:
(3)
•O2 − scavenging rate (%) = [(A1-A2)/(A1-A0)] × 100 %
Herein, A0 is the absorbance of INT, A1 and A2 are the absorbance at 505 nm in the absence and presence of nanozymes, respectively.

SOD-POD-like cascade catalytic activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes: Xanthine reacts with xanthine oxidase in a phosphate buffer at room temperature to produce •O2 −, and then nanozymes were added. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a NaOAc/HOAc buffer (pH 3.5), followed by the addition of TMB. After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, the UV–vis spectrum is determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes for enhanced antibacterial activity
FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with POD-like activity for enhanced antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro antibacterial properties of the tested agents was assessed using the broth microdilution method according to the CLSI guidelines [24]. The initial bacterial solution (E.coli ATCC 25922) was diluted to 1 × 105 CFU/mL for antibacterial experiments. Then the 100 μL bacterial solution was combined with H2O2 (0.016 mM), sodium acetate buffer and different types of nanozymes. The pH of the reaction system was 4.5. After incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, 10-fold serial dilutions of the mixture in the microtubes was plated onto M − H agar medium and incubated at 37 °C for 16 h. Synergistic effect is defined as an Fractional Inhibitory Concentration (FIC) index≦0.5 [25–27]. The FIC index was calculated using the formula:

Where A0 is the number of colonies in the control (medium alone) and A1 is the number of colonies in the experimental group. All experiments were repeated at least three times.
Consistent with the above methods, the strain was changed to S. aureus (ATCC 25923) and H2O2 concentration was changed to 0.08 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with HPO-like activity for enhanced antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: The initial bacterial solution (E.coli ATCC 25922) was diluted to 1 × 105 CFU/mL for antibacterial experiments. Then the 100 μL bacterial solution was combined with H2O2 (0.2 mM), KBr/KCl (25 mM) and different types of nanozymes. After incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, 10-fold serial dilutions of the mixture in the microtubes was plated onto M − H agar medium and incubated at 37 °C for 16 h.
The experimental procedure of antibacterial activity against S. aureus was the same as that of E.coli.
All numerical data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Origin (version 2024) and Graphpad Prism were used for statistical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with multi-enzyme for enhanced antibacterial activity
Synthesis, phase and morphology of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-metals ions serve as precursors to synthesize FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes via deposition and phosphorization, as described in Fig. 1 a. Initially, CDs-Fe3+/CDs-Cu2+ chelates were formed by introducing Fe3+ and Cu2+ into EDTA functionalized CDs and thoroughly stirring the mixture. Following this, an alkaline solution was added to precipitate the chelates, resulting in the formation of CDs-Fe(OH)3/CDs-Cu(OH)2 (Fe–Cu DHs/CDs) precipitates. Subsequently, NaH2PO2 served as the phosphorus source for the pyrolysis reaction. During the phosphorization process, CDs-Fe(OH)3/CDs-Cu(OH)2 converted into FeCu–P/CDs with an intimate interface. Concurrently, dehydration condensation reactions between functional groups on the surface of CDs facilitated a structural transition from 0D nanoparticles to 2D nanosheets [28]. The synthesis of other metal phosphide/CDs composites can be carried out using a similar technology. The existence of CDs-Fe3+/CDs-Cu2+ chelates was confirmed by fluorescence quenching of CDs (Fig. S1) and FTIR spectra (Fig. S2). These analytical techniques collectively confirmed the formation of CDs-Fe3+/CDs-Cu2+ precursors, which are crucial for subsequent analysis of the strong electron interaction between CDs and FeCu–P.

XRD patterns were further used to characterize the phase of FeCu–P/CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, the XRD pattern of FeCu–P/CDs corresponds to the standard cards of JCPDS no. 71–2262 (FeP), 85–1326 (Cu) and 71–2261 (Cu3P). The result shows FeCu–P/CDs are composed of three phases. Additionally, the XRD patterns of FeCu–P, Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs were shown in Fig. S3. The morphology and microstructure of FeCu–P/CDs were characterized by TEM and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping. As exhibited in Fig. 1c, FeCu–P/CDs appear as monodisperse spheres with an obvious carbon layer. TEM images of FeCu–P (Fig. S4) show irregular morphology and obvious accumulation. The results showed that the introduction of CDs effectively refined the nanostructure of metal phosphide, so as to provide more active sites for catalytic reaction and improve catalytic activity. The lattice spacing of 0.266 nm, 0.240 nm and 0.249 nm correspond to the (211) plane of FeP, (111) plane of Cu and (300) plane of Cu3P, respectively (Fig. 1d). The results are in agreement with the XRD, further confirming the successful synthesis of FeCu–P/CDs. Moreover, Cu, Fe, P and N elements mapping show these elements are uniformly and adequately distributed on the entire FeCu–P/CDs (Fig. 1e). Collectively, the XRD, TEM, and EDS elemental mapping images substantiate the successful synthesis of FeCu–P/CDs.

XPS measurements were performed on FeCu–P/CDs, Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs to investigate the electronic interactions between Fe and Cu. The existence of Fe, P, O, N and C was confirmed by XPS survey spectra of FeCu–P/CDs (Fig. S5). The Fe 2p XPS spectra of FeCu–P/CDs (Fig. 1f) show two prominent peaks in 711.2/724.5 eV and 712.9/726.7 eV, which are attributed to the Fe2+ 2p3/2/Fe2+ 2p1/2 and Fe3+ 2p3/2/Fe3+ 2p1/2, respectively [29,30]. The peaks at 707.1 and 720.6 eV belong to the Fe–P bond, further confirming the existence of FeP in FeCu–P/CDs [31]. The Cu 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 1g) reveal the electron binding energies of metallic Cu at 932.3 and 952.4 eV, exhibiting the presence of Cu+/Cu0 2p3/2 and Cu+/Cu0 2p1/2 in FeCu–P/CDs, respectively [32]. Additionally, the peaks at 934.6 and 954.5 eV can be attributed to Cu2+ [33,34]. The high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 1h) of Fe–P/CDs displays a peak at 133.6 eV, which is assigned to P–O bond, while the peaks at 130.5 and 129.6 eV correspond to the Metal-P bond [35–37]. Importantly, a negative shift in the Cu 2p binding energy is observed in FeCu–P/CDs relative to Cu–P/CDs. It shows that the chemical environment of Cu in FeCu–P/CDs is significantly different from that in Cu–P/CDs. The 3d electronic structure of Cu is relatively active, and it is easy to have electron transfer or charge redistribution due to changes in the chemical environment. This indicates the electron cloud distribution of Cu element in FeCu–P/CDs is affected by Fe element, resulting in the change of its binding energy, resulting in a certain electronic interaction between Fe and Cu elements [38]. Furthermore, high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s and O 1s of FeCu–P/CDs compared to CDs, exhibit new peaks attributed to Metal-N and Metal-O bonds, indicating the interactions between Fe/Cu and CDs (Fig. S6a and b). Additionally, the Fe 2p and Cu 2p peaks in FeCu–P/CDs are shifted compared to those in FeCu–P, further confirming the strong electron interactions between FeCu–P and CDs (Fig. S6c and d). This electron transfer will have a profound impact on the electronic properties and overall catalytic activities of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes. Zeta potentials of FeCu–P/CDs, Fe–P/CDs, and Cu–P/CDs are −33.2 mV, −31.5 mV, and −29.9 mV, respectively. The result show that all samples possess excellent stability, with FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibiting superior stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with POD-like activity for enhanced antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: A 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) colorimetric assay was used to assess the POD-like catalytic activities of various nanozymes (Fig. 2 a). As depicted in Fig. 2b, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrated excellent POD-like catalytic activity compared with FeCu–P, Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs nanozymes. Specifically, the POD-like specific activity of FeCu–P/CDs is 37.9 U/mg, which is significantly higher than that of FeCu–P, Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs (Fig. 2c). Notably, the POD-like specific activity of FeCu–P/CDs is improved 0.9 and 8.2 times than that of Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs, respectively. The catalytic activity of various nanozymes on TMB and H2O2 was investigated by Michaelis-Menten kinetics. The V m and K m values of FeCu–P/CDs for TMB are 4.26 × 10−6 M s−1 and 0.27 mM, respectively. The V m and K m values of FeCu–P/CDs with H2O2 as substrate are 1.42 × 10−6 M s−1 and 0.16 mM, respectively (Fig. 2d and e). Compared with other nanozymes in this work (Fig. 2f, g and Fig. S7), FeCu–P/CDs demonstrate remarkable catalytic reactivity and affinity for both TMB and H2O2, indicating FeCu–P/CDs possess the best POD-like catalytic activity than other nanozymes. The affinities of FeCu–P/CDs to TMB and H2O2 are relatively high compared with HRP and other reported nanozymes (Table S1). Compared with other reported POD-like nanozymes, FeCu–P/CDs show competitive specific activity (Fig. 2h) [39–51]. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrate excellent stability, with their relative activities showing no significant decline after 20 cycles or 60 days of storage (Fig. S8a and b). Additionally, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit pH- and temperature-dependent catalytic behavior similar to HRP (Fig. S8c and d), with an optimal reaction pH of 3.5. These results collectively indicate that FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes possess excellent POD-like catalytic activity.

Subsequently, the reaction mechanism of H2O2 catalyzed by FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes was investigated. Isopropanol, a common ‧OH trapping agent, is used to verify the formation of ‧OH in the reaction system. As illustrated in Fig. 2i, the introduction of isopropanol results in a progressive decline in the absorbance at 652 nm, indicating the generation of ·OH within the reaction system. As shown in the EPR spectrum of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes + H2O2 system (Fig. 2j), the ‧OH signal further directly indicates the generation of ‧OH [6]. Therefore, the possible reaction mechanism of H2O2 catalyzed by FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes is as follows (Fig. 2k) [21,52]: First, H2O2 molecules are adsorbed at metal sites in FeCu–P/CDs and activated. The activated H2O2 molecules dissociate evenly, forming two hydroxyl groups at the metal site. Subsequently, one of the hydroxyl groups is desorbed from the adsorption site to form ·OH. In an acidic environment, the other adsorbed hydroxyl groups react with protonated hydrogen atoms to form adsorbed H2O molecules. Finally, after the desorption of H2O molecules, the catalytic center of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes active site returned to the initial state. In this way, the reaction system completes a POD-like enzyme catalytic cycle.

Nanozymes with POD-like activity have been demonstrated the ability to decompose H2O2 into highly reactive ·OH, which can damage bacterial cell membranes and induce apoptosis. Therefore, E. coli and S. aureus were used as Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, respectively, to investigate the antibacterial activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with excellent POD-like activity in an acidic environment. As shown in Fig. 3 a, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes effectively catalyze the conversion of H2O2 into ·OH, which is toxic to bacterial cells, effectively killing the bacteria as a result. In the presence of only H2O2 and FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes, the antibacterial rates for E. coli are 14.9 % and 50.7 % respectively. In contrast, when the two components are combined, the antibacterial rate increases significantly to 88.1 % (Fig. 3b and c), which is better than that of Cu–P/CDs (41.0 %), Fe–P/CDs (45.8 %) and FeCu–P (55.6 %) nanozymes (Fig. S9a and b). Similarly, in the presence of H2O2 or FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes, the antibacterial rates of H2O2 and FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes for S. aureusas are 13.6 % and 14.4 %, respectively. In the presence of H2O2, the antibacterial rate of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes increases significantly to 87.9 % (Fig. 3b and c). The antibacterial rate of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes is better than that of Cu–P/CDs (17.1 %), Fe–P/CDs (36.2 %) and FeCu–P (43.7 %) nanozymes (Fig. S9a and c). Further, take E. coli as an example, the fractional inhibitory concentration (FIC, see Formula 4) value < 0.025 mg/mL/0.4 mg/mL+0.016 mM/4 mM = 0.0665, indicating there is a synergistic antibacterial effect between FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes and H2O2. These results reveal that FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes can eliminate bacteria through POD-like catalytic activity. Importantly, the antibacterial activity is depended on concentration of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes (Fig. 3d). At concentrations of 25 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL, the antibacterial rates against both E. coli and S. aureus exceeded 99.9 %, approaching about 100 % (Fig. 3e and f). Additionally, using E. coli as model bacteria, the antibacterial rates of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes in different pH conditions were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S10, the antibacterial rate gradually decreased as the pH increased from 4.5 to 6.5. Moreover, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes demonstrate a pronounced advantage in antibacterial properties, demonstrating a significantly enhanced antibacterial activity compared to other classes of POD-like nanozymes (Fig. S11) [53–63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with HPO-like activity for enhanced antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: HPO-like enzymes catalyze the reaction between H2O2 and halogen ions (such as chlorine, bromine and iodine) to generate hypohalous acids. Hypohalous acids with potent oxidizing properties can be used for the elimination of bacteria. Herein, the celestine blue (CB) assay was employed to assess the HPO-like activity of FeCu–P/CDs (Fig. 4 a). As depicted in Fig. 4b and c, all the nanozymes exhibit HPO-like catalytic activity, with FeCu–P/CDs demonstrating the most pronounced catalytic activity. The results indicate FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes can effectively catalyze the conversion of Br− into HBrO. Fig. 4d shows the time-dependent activity, which is very consistent with typical Michaelis-Menten dynamics. Therefore, the steady-state dynamics of these nanozymes are investigated. In particular, the K m of FeCu–P/CDs for Br−, CB and H2O2 reaches 0.93 mM, 0.092 mM and 0.19 mM, respectively. The V m of FeCu–P/CDs for Br−, CB and H2O2 is 0.95 × 10−5 M s−1, 2.19 × 10−5 M s−1 and 2.01 × 10−5 M s−1, respectively (Fig. 4e–g). Compared with other nanozymes, FeCu–P/CDs exhibit the highest V m (Fig. 4h and Fig. S12). Based on the published literature [64,65], the HPO-like catalytic mechanism of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes is speculated. First, H2O2 coordinates with the metal active site on the surface of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes. This state is unstable, and H2O2 tends to dissociate into two OH∗. Then, one of the OH∗ reacts with Br− to form HBrO. The other OH∗ will be protonated to form H2O∗. After the HBrO and H2O molecules are released, the catalytic cycle is completed (Fig. 4i).

HPO-like enzymes catalyze the reaction between H2O2 and Br−/Cl− to generate highly oxidizing HBrO/HClO, which are active halogen compounds with significant oxidation ability. These compounds have potential applications in the elimination of bacteria. Therefore, the antibacterial rates of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with HPO-like catalytic activity were investigated using E. coli and S. aureus as Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial model in a neutral environment (Fig. 5 a). As depicted in Fig. 5b and c, the antibacterial activities for E. coli are not significant when the system contains only a single component or a combination of two components (0.5 % and 35.5 %, respectively). In contrast, when the composition of the antibacterial reaction system is increased to three (FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes, Br− and H2O2), the antibacterial rate is significantly enhanced to 89.3 % at 200 μg/mL, which is better than that of Cu–P/CDs (66.3 %) and FeCu–P (70.2 %) nanozymes (Fig. S13). This phenomenon indicates FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes facilitate the conversion of Br− into HBrO through the catalytic action of HPO-like enzymes, thus playing an antibacterial activity. As shown in Fig. 5b and d, when the system contains only a single component (FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes) or a combination of these two components (Br− + H2O2), the antibacterial activities for S. aureus are 16.1 % and 24.9 %, respectively. When the composition of the antibacterial reaction system is increased to three (FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with Br− and H2O2), the antibacterial rate of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes is significantly enhanced to 68.5 % at 200 μg/mL, which is better than that of Cu–P/CDs (35.9 %) and FeCu–P (44.7 %) nanozymes (Fig. S13). The enhanced antibacterial activity can be attributed to the electronic interactions between Fe–P and Cu–P, or between the FeCu–P and CDs substrate in FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes. These interactions boost the HPO-like catalytic activity, which in turn improves the overall antibacterial activity. Furthermore, the antibacterial activity of HPO-like enzymes catalyzed reaction was investigated when Cl− replaced Br−. As shown in Fig. S14a, b and d, the system demonstrates antibacterial activity (84.1 %) that is comparable to those of Br−. Notably, the antibacterial rate for E. coli of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes achieves 98.7 % at a concentration of 400 μg/mL (Fig. S14c and d). Additionally, the biocompatibility of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes was evaluated using the MTT assay, which indicate FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit good biocompatibility (Fig. S15).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with SOD-POD cascade catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit a SOD-POD-like cascade catalytic activity (Fig. 6 a). Initially, the SOD-like catalytic activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes was investigated. The absorption at 505 nm in the reaction system was measured using xanthine and xanthine oxidase to generate •O2 −, with INT serving as an indicator. As shown in Fig. 6b, the absorbance at 505 nm gradually diminishes with the increasing concentrations of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes. This indicates that the FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes possess SOD-like catalytic activity. At a concentration of 0.167 μg mL−1, the elimination rate of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes for •O2 − exceeds 85 % (Fig. 6c), demonstrating the SOD-like catalytic activity.

Notably, FeCu–P/CDs exhibit a low IC50 value of 0.037 μg/mL, which is lower than that reported for other works and for Cu–P/CDs (0.056 μg/mL) and FeCu–P (0.064 μg/mL) (Fig. 6d and Fig. S16), indicating the superior SOD-like catalytic activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes [66–70]. Further, the SOD-POD-like cascade catalytic activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes was investigated. The solution of •O2 − + FeCu–P/CDs + TMB exhibit a typical strong absorbance at 652 nm, indicative of the successful coupling of SOD-like and POD-like activity into an enzymatic cascade (Fig. 6e). Additionally, EPR spectra have shown that during the catalytic process of FeCu–P/CDs on •O2 −, ·OH is generated as a result of the SOD-POD-like cascade reaction (Fig. 6f), further confirming the SOD-POD-like cascade catalytic activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes.

In summary, this work reported FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes with multi-enzyme activity to catalyze ROS production for enhanced antibacterial activity. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes show POD-like, HPO-like and SOD-like catalytic activities, as well as SOD-POD-like cascade catalytic activity. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibit excellent POD-like catalytic activity with a specific activity of 37.9 U/mg, which is enhanced 0.9 and 8.2 times than that of Fe–P/CDs and Cu–P/CDs, respectively. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes can efficiently generate ROS (·OH and HBrO/HClO) through dual-enzyme catalytic activity, and realize the dual-pathway elimination of bacteria. FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes have demonstrated well antibacterial effect on E. coli and S. aureus, which can achieve more than 99.9 % antibacterial rate at 25 μg/mL and 80 μg/mL, respectively, via the POD-like catalytic pathway in an acidic environment. The antibacterial activity of FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes was significantly enhanced compared with Cu–P/CDs and Fe–P/CDs. Based on HPO-like catalytic pathway, FeCu–P/CDs nanozymes exhibited antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus, reaching an antibacterial rate of 98.7 % against E. coli at a concentration of 400 μg/mL in a neutral environment. This work will inspire the exploration of various of bimetallic species-phosphate/carbon nanomaterials with high catalytic activity to enhance antibacterial properties via multi-enzyme activity pathway.
